title: The Errors Worth Keeping
summary: An interview with an oral historian in Kraków who argues that the inaccuracies in people's memories are not noise to be corrected but data of a different and often more revealing kind.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: You've spent thirty years recording the testimonies of ordinary people. Critics sometimes say oral history is unreliable — that memory distorts, that people misremember dates and embellish. How do you answer that?

body.speaker: Dr Wójcik
body.text: I don't answer it, because the criticism rests on a category error. It assumes my job is to extract accurate facts from a faulty container. But a person's memory is not a faulty filing cabinet. It's a living interpretation, constantly revised in light of everything that came after. When someone misremembers a date, the historian who only notes the error has missed the interesting thing, which is why the mind chose to file the event there. A memory is less like a photograph than like a house that has been lived in for fifty years: every occupant has moved the furniture, repainted a wall, knocked through a doorway. To complain that it no longer matches the original blueprint is to misunderstand what a house is for.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: But surely facts still matter. If a witness places an event in the wrong year, that undermines the account.

body.speaker: Dr Wójcik
body.text: It undermines the account as a source of dates. It does nothing of the kind to the account as a source of meaning. Let me give you a real example. A woman once described, in vivid detail, soldiers marching past her childhood home on a day that, documentary records prove, they could not possibly have marched. The literal-minded conclusion is that her memory failed. But what she had done, without knowing it, was fuse two separate terrors into a single image — and that fusion told me more about how fear organises a child's world than any correct chronology ever could.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: Isn't there a danger, though, that you end up treating every falsehood as a kind of poetic truth? At some point, surely, wrong is just wrong.

body.speaker: Dr Wójcik
body.text: You're right to press me on that, and it's where careless practitioners go astray. There's a real difference between a meaningful distortion and a simple mistake, and also between an honest error and a deliberate lie. I'm not saying anything goes. If someone claims a factory existed that the records show never did, I don't shrug and call it their truth. I cross-check relentlessly. The point is not that facts don't matter — it's that the errors, once you've identified them as errors, become a second layer of evidence rather than a reason to discard the testimony. The skill is knowing which is which, and that takes years. A novice hears a contradiction and either panics or shrugs; an experienced interviewer hears it and gets curious, because the contradiction is usually pointing at something the witness can't say directly.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: Give me the principle, then. How do you decide which errors are worth keeping?

body.speaker: Dr Wójcik
body.text: An error is worth keeping when it is systematic rather than random — when many people misremember in the same direction. If a dozen unrelated witnesses all shift the same event earlier in time, or all attribute a decision to a figure who wasn't responsible, that consistent distortion is a fingerprint. It's the shape of a collective need: a need to assign blame, or to make sense of chaos, or to protect a self-image. The individual error is noise. The shared error is signal.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: That's a striking inversion. So the more people get it wrong in the same way, the more valuable the mistake becomes.

body.speaker: Dr Wójcik
body.text: Precisely. And there's a humbling lesson in it for the documentary historians who look down on us. They trust the written record because it feels solid. But a document is also a memory — somebody's memory, written down at a particular moment, for a particular purpose, with its own distortions baked in. The difference is only that the document doesn't blush, and can't be asked why. I'd rather have a witness I can question than an archive that has quietly forgotten it was ever anyone's point of view.

bóp méo, làm sai lệch
tô vẽ, thêm thắt cho hay

kết luận theo nghĩa đen, máy móc

annotations.phrase: with its own distortions baked in
annotations.meaningVi: với những sai lệch riêng đã được cài sẵn bên trong
tài liệu không biết đỏ mặt (không lộ ra dối trá)

grammarNotes.title: Parallelism with negation-then-affirmation
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Cấu trúc 'not X — it's Y' (phủ định rồi khẳng định song song) là công cụ lập luận sắc bén ở C1. Dr Wójcik dùng nhiều lần: 'The individual error is noise. The shared error is signal.' và 'The point is not that facts don't matter — it's that the errors ... become a second layer of evidence.' Phép song hành cú pháp làm hai vế đối lập trở nên cân xứng, dễ nhớ, và buộc người nghe cảm nhận sự tương phản chứ không chỉ được kể về nó.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Watch the negation-then-affirmation parallelism: 'The point is not that facts don't matter — it's that the errors ... become a second layer of evidence.' and the tight antithesis 'The individual error is noise. The shared error is signal.' The matched grammar of the two halves makes the contrast feel inevitable and memorable — a precise rhetorical tool for redirecting a misreading toward the speaker's real claim.

translationVi:
Interviewer: Ông đã dành ba mươi năm ghi lại lời kể của những người bình thường. Những người phê bình đôi khi nói rằng lịch sử truyền miệng không đáng tin cậy — rằng trí nhớ bóp méo, rằng người ta nhớ sai ngày tháng và thêm thắt. Ông trả lời điều đó thế nào?
Dr Wójcik: Tôi không trả lời nó, vì lời phê bình đó dựa trên một lỗi nhầm phạm trù. Nó cho rằng công việc của tôi là rút ra các sự kiện chính xác từ một vật chứa bị lỗi. Nhưng trí nhớ của một người không phải là một tủ hồ sơ bị lỗi. Nó là một sự diễn giải sống động, liên tục được sửa đổi dưới ánh sáng của tất cả những gì đã đến sau. Khi ai đó nhớ sai một ngày, sử gia chỉ ghi nhận sai sót đã bỏ lỡ điều thú vị, đó là vì sao tâm trí lại chọn lưu sự kiện ấy ở đó. Một ký ức ít giống một tấm ảnh hơn là giống một ngôi nhà đã có người ở suốt năm mươi năm: mỗi người ở đều đã xê dịch đồ đạc, sơn lại một bức tường, đục thông một ô cửa. Phàn nàn rằng nó không còn khớp với bản thiết kế gốc là hiểu sai mục đích của một ngôi nhà.
Interviewer: Nhưng chắc chắn sự kiện vẫn quan trọng. Nếu một nhân chứng đặt một sự kiện vào sai năm, điều đó làm suy yếu lời kể.
Dr Wójcik: Nó làm suy yếu lời kể với tư cách là một nguồn ngày tháng. Nó chẳng làm gì như thế với lời kể với tư cách là một nguồn ý nghĩa. Để tôi cho cô một ví dụ thật. Một người phụ nữ từng mô tả, với chi tiết sống động, những người lính diễu hành qua ngôi nhà thời thơ ấu của bà vào một ngày mà, theo hồ sơ tư liệu chứng minh, họ không thể nào diễu hành được. Kết luận theo nghĩa đen là trí nhớ của bà đã hỏng. Nhưng điều bà đã làm, mà không biết, là hợp nhất hai nỗi kinh hoàng riêng biệt thành một hình ảnh duy nhất — và sự hợp nhất đó cho tôi biết nhiều hơn về cách nỗi sợ tổ chức thế giới của một đứa trẻ so với bất kỳ trình tự thời gian chính xác nào.
Interviewer: Nhưng chẳng phải có nguy cơ rằng ông rốt cuộc coi mọi điều sai như một dạng sự thật thi ca sao? Đến một lúc nào đó, chắc chắn, sai là sai.
Dr Wójcik: Cô đúng khi gặng hỏi tôi về điều đó, và đó là chỗ những người làm nghề cẩu thả đi lạc lối. Có một sự khác biệt thật sự giữa một sự bóp méo có ý nghĩa và một sai sót đơn giản, và cũng giữa một lỗi trung thực và một lời nói dối có chủ ý. Tôi không nói cái gì cũng được. Nếu ai đó tuyên bố có một nhà máy mà hồ sơ cho thấy chưa bao giờ tồn tại, tôi không nhún vai và gọi đó là sự thật của họ. Tôi đối chiếu chéo không ngừng. Vấn đề không phải là sự kiện không quan trọng — mà là các sai sót, một khi cô đã xác định chúng là sai sót, trở thành một lớp bằng chứng thứ hai chứ không phải lý do để vứt bỏ lời chứng. Kỹ năng nằm ở chỗ biết cái nào là cái nào, và điều đó mất nhiều năm. Người mới nghe thấy một mâu thuẫn thì hoặc hoảng hốt hoặc nhún vai; người phỏng vấn dày dạn nghe nó thì thấy tò mò, vì mâu thuẫn ấy thường đang chỉ về một điều mà nhân chứng không thể nói thẳng ra.
Interviewer: Vậy hãy cho tôi nguyên tắc. Ông quyết định thế nào những sai sót nào đáng giữ?
Dr Wójcik: Một sai sót đáng giữ khi nó có hệ thống chứ không phải ngẫu nhiên — khi nhiều người nhớ sai theo cùng một hướng. Nếu một chục nhân chứng không liên quan đến nhau đều dịch chuyển cùng một sự kiện sớm hơn về thời gian, hoặc đều quy một quyết định cho một nhân vật không chịu trách nhiệm, sự bóp méo nhất quán đó là một dấu vân tay. Đó là hình dạng của một nhu cầu tập thể: nhu cầu quy tội, hoặc lý giải sự hỗn loạn, hoặc bảo vệ một hình ảnh bản thân. Sai sót cá nhân là nhiễu. Sai sót chung là tín hiệu.
Interviewer: Đó là một sự đảo ngược đáng kinh ngạc. Vậy càng nhiều người sai theo cùng một cách, sai sót càng trở nên có giá trị.
Dr Wójcik: Chính xác. Và có một bài học khiêm nhường trong đó cho những sử gia tư liệu khinh chúng tôi. Họ tin vào văn bản viết vì nó có vẻ vững chắc. Nhưng một tài liệu cũng là một ký ức — ký ức của ai đó, được viết xuống vào một thời điểm cụ thể, vì một mục đích cụ thể, với những sai lệch riêng đã được cài sẵn bên trong. Khác biệt chỉ là tài liệu không biết đỏ mặt, và không thể hỏi tại sao. Tôi thà có một nhân chứng tôi có thể chất vấn còn hơn một kho lưu trữ đã lặng lẽ quên rằng nó từng là quan điểm của một ai đó.

questions.prompt: Why does Dr Wójcik refuse to 'answer' the criticism that oral history is unreliable?
options: Because he agrees that oral history is unreliable
Because the criticism rests on a category error about what his job is
Because he has never encountered the criticism before
Because documentary records always settle the matter
questions.explanation: 'I don't answer it, because the criticism rests on a category error. It assumes my job is to extract accurate facts from a faulty container.'
questions.hint: His first answer names the flaw in the criticism.

questions.prompt: What does Dr Wójcik say a person's memory actually is?
options: A living interpretation, constantly revised in light of everything that came after
A perfect recording of past events
A deliberate construction designed to mislead historians
questions.explanation: 'a person's memory is not a faulty filing cabinet. It's a living interpretation, constantly revised in light of everything that came after.'
questions.hint: His first answer offers an alternative model of memory.

questions.prompt: How does Dr Wójcik respond to the claim that a wrong date 'undermines the account'?
options: He agrees the whole account should be discarded
He says it undermines the account as a source of dates but not as a source of meaning
He says dates never matter in history
He says the witness must have been lying
questions.explanation: 'It undermines the account as a source of dates. It does nothing of the kind to the account as a source of meaning.'
questions.hint: He distinguishes two kinds of source.

questions.prompt: What did the woman's 'impossible' memory of marching soldiers reveal, according to Dr Wójcik?
options: That she was deliberately lying for sympathy
That she had fused two separate terrors into one image, revealing how fear organises a child's world
That documentary records were themselves wrong
That soldiers had marched twice that year
questions.explanation: 'she had ... fuse[d] two separate terrors into a single image — and that fusion told me more about how fear organises a child's world.'
questions.hint: His second long answer gives this example.

questions.prompt: How does Dr Wójcik respond to the worry that he treats every falsehood as 'poetic truth'?
options: He admits anything goes in his method
He agrees it is a real danger and insists he distinguishes meaningful distortions from simple mistakes and cross-checks relentlessly
He says facts never matter to him
He refuses to engage with the question
questions.explanation: 'You're right to press me on that ... I'm not saying anything goes ... I cross-check relentlessly.'
questions.hint: He concedes the danger before defending his standards.

questions.prompt: According to Dr Wójcik, what happens to errors once they are identified as errors?
options: They become a second layer of evidence rather than a reason to discard the testimony
They must be deleted from the record
They prove the witness cannot be trusted
questions.explanation: 'the errors, once you've identified them as errors, become a second layer of evidence rather than a reason to discard the testimony.'
questions.hint: He reframes the role of identified errors.

questions.prompt: What is Dr Wójcik's principle for which errors are 'worth keeping'?
options: Errors made by the most educated witnesses
Errors that are systematic rather than random — when many people misremember in the same direction
Errors that match the documentary record
questions.explanation: 'An error is worth keeping when it is systematic rather than random — when many people misremember in the same direction.'
questions.hint: He answers the request for a principle directly.

questions.prompt: What does a 'consistent distortion' across many witnesses reveal, in his view?
options: The shape of a collective need — to assign blame, make sense of chaos, or protect a self-image
That the documentary record is unreliable
questions.explanation: 'that consistent distortion is a fingerprint. It's the shape of a collective need: a need to assign blame, or to make sense of chaos.'
questions.hint: He calls the shared distortion 'a fingerprint'.

questions.prompt: The contrast 'The individual error is noise. The shared error is signal' means that:
options: A single person's mistake is random, but the same mistake across many people carries real information
Errors should always be removed from records
Witnesses should not be interviewed individually
questions.explanation: Random individual error is 'noise'; the systematic shared error is 'signal' — meaningful data about a collective need.
questions.hint: Connect 'systematic rather than random' to the noise/signal pair.

questions.prompt: What 'humbling lesson' does Dr Wójcik offer documentary historians?
options: That written records are always more reliable than memory
That a document is also a memory, written for a purpose, with its own distortions baked in
That oral history should replace documents entirely
questions.explanation: 'a document is also a memory — somebody's memory, written down at a particular moment, for a particular purpose, with its own distortions baked in.'
questions.hint: His final answer turns the criticism back on documents.

questions.prompt: Why does Dr Wójcik prefer 'a witness I can question' to an archive?
options: Because witnesses always tell the truth
Because the document doesn't blush and can't be asked why, having forgotten it was ever a point of view
Because archives are too expensive to maintain
Because witnesses remember more dates accurately
questions.explanation: 'the document doesn't blush, and can't be asked why. I'd rather have a witness I can question than an archive that has quietly forgotten it was ever anyone's point of view.'
questions.hint: His final answer contrasts questioning a person with reading a document.

questions.prompt: Which paraphrase is closest to Dr Wójcik's overall stance?
options: Facts are irrelevant to history; only feelings matter
Memory errors, once identified, are valuable evidence about meaning and collective need, though facts must still be cross-checked
Oral history is unreliable and should be abandoned
Documents are always superior to spoken testimony
questions.explanation: He insists 'I'm not saying anything goes' and cross-checks, yet treats systematic errors as a 'second layer of evidence' about meaning.
questions.hint: Look for the option that keeps both fact-checking and the value of errors.

questions.prompt: The phrase 'a category error' (Dr Wójcik) means:
options: A mistake in spelling or grammar
A mistake of treating something as the wrong kind of thing
An error that recurs in many witnesses
questions.explanation: He says the criticism wrongly assumes his job is to extract facts from a 'faulty container' — i.e., it mis-classifies what memory is.
questions.hint: Think about misclassifying the kind of thing memory is.

questions.prompt: What does 'with its own distortions baked in' suggest about documents?
options: That documents are heated during production
That documents contain built-in biases from their moment and purpose, just as memories do
That documents cannot be read accurately
questions.explanation: A document is 'somebody's memory ... for a particular purpose, with its own distortions baked in' — biases built in from the start.
questions.hint: Connect 'baked in' to the idea that documents are also memories.

questions.prompt: Which claim does Dr Wójcik NOT make?
options: Systematic errors across witnesses can reveal a collective need
Any falsehood a witness states should be accepted as their personal truth
He cross-checks claims against the records
questions.explanation: He explicitly rejects this: 'I'm not saying anything goes ... I don't shrug and call it their truth. I cross-check relentlessly.'
questions.hint: Recall his response to the 'poetic truth' worry.

cloze.template:
Interviewer: Critics sometimes say oral history is unreliable — that memory distorts, that people misremember dates and {{b1}}. How do you answer that?
Dr Wójcik: I don't answer it, because the criticism rests on a {{b2}} error. It assumes my job is to extract accurate facts from a faulty container. But a person's memory is not a faulty filing cabinet. It's a living {{b3}}, constantly revised in light of everything that came after.
Interviewer: But surely facts still matter. If a witness places an event in the wrong year, that {{b4}} the account.
Dr Wójcik: It undermines the account as a source of dates. It does nothing of the kind to the account as a source of {{b5}}. A woman once described soldiers marching past her childhood home on a day they could not possibly have marched. What she had done, without knowing it, was {{b6}} two separate terrors into a single image — and that fusion told me more about how fear organises a child's world than any correct {{b7}} ever could.
Interviewer: Isn't there a danger that you treat every falsehood as a kind of poetic truth?
Dr Wójcik: You're right to {{b8}} me on that, and it's where careless practitioners go astray. I'm not saying anything goes. If someone claims a factory existed that the records show never did, I don't shrug and call it their truth. I cross-check {{b9}}. The errors, once you've identified them as errors, become a second layer of {{b10}} rather than a reason to discard the testimony.
Interviewer: How do you decide which errors are worth keeping?
Dr Wójcik: An error is worth keeping when it is {{b11}} rather than random — when many people misremember in the same direction. If a dozen unrelated witnesses all shift the same event earlier in time, that consistent distortion is a {{b12}}. The individual error is {{b13}}. The shared error is signal.
Interviewer: So the more people get it wrong in the same way, the more valuable the mistake becomes.
Dr Wójcik: Precisely. A document is also a memory — somebody's memory, written down for a particular purpose, with its own distortions {{b14}} in. The difference is only that the document doesn't blush. I'd rather have a witness I can {{b15}} than an archive that has quietly forgotten it was ever anyone's point of view.

blanks.explanation: 'misremember dates and embellish' — add invented or exaggerated details.
'a category error' — treating something as the wrong kind of thing.
'a living interpretation' — the model of memory he proposes.
'that undermines the account' — the interviewer's verb, which he then qualifies.
'as a source of meaning' — set against 'a source of dates'.
'fuse two separate terrors into a single image' — melt them together; his chosen verb.
'than any correct chronology ever could' — the ordering of events in time.
'right to press me on that' — to push him hard on a point.
'I cross-check relentlessly' — without letting up; his exact adverb.
'a second layer of evidence' — the reframing of identified errors.
'systematic rather than random' — following a consistent pattern.
'that consistent distortion is a fingerprint' — a distinctive identifying trace.
'The individual error is noise' — meaningless signal interference, set against 'signal'.
'with its own distortions baked in' — the idiom for being built in from the start.
'a witness I can question' — interrogate and ask 'why', unlike a silent document.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Dr Wójcik argues that when many unrelated people misremember the same event in the same way, the shared error becomes valuable 'signal' about a collective need. Can you think of a widely shared belief or memory in your own community or country that is factually shaky but reveals something true about what people need to believe? What would be lost if you simply corrected it?
